docs.title: Welcome to CoRE Stack
docs.text: An open geospatial backend for democratic planning, public data access, and computation pipelines.

docs.title: Start in the Browser
docs.text:
You do not need a local install to start exploring CoRE Stack.

  • Public Swagger: https://geoserver.core-stack.org/swagger/
  • Local Swagger after install: http://127.0.0.1:8000/swagger/
  • Main backend route map: nrm_app/urls.py

Two good first calls:

curl https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_states/

curl "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_districts/29/"

These auth-free geography endpoints are implemented in geoadmin/api.py and routed through geoadmin/urls.py.

Use Search Early

If you are busy, use the search button in the header and jump straight to terms like NDVI, LULC, hydrology, Koraput, GeoServer, gee_account_id, or facilities proximity.

docs.title: What Exists Today
docs.text:
CoRE Stack already exposes three useful surfaces:

  • Auth-free geographic discovery for states, districts, blocks, and proposed blocks in geoadmin/api.py
  • API-key public data access for admin lookups, MWS data, generated layer URLs, reports, and geometries in public_api/api.py
  • Task-submission compute endpoints for SWB, LULC, hydrology, terrain, and related pipelines in computing/urls.py

The public-data layer URL builder lives in public_api/views.py, and the compute handlers mostly queue background work from computing/api.py.

docs.title: Installation Path
docs.text:
The backend already ships with an automated installer in installation/install.sh. It currently automates:

  • Miniconda installation
  • Conda environment creation
  • PostgreSQL setup
  • Apache setup
  • backend clone and deploy directory setup
  • .env generation
  • static collection and migrations

The current backend install guide still calls out manual prerequisites and follow-up such as RabbitMQ and environment configuration in installation/INSTALLATION.md.

docs.title: Option 1: Explore the Live API
docs.text:
Start with live Swagger and the API docs in this repo:

  • Swagger UI
  • Public Endpoints

docs.title: Option 2: Install Locally
docs.text:
Use the backend installer, then run the local Django server:

git clone https://github.com/core-stack-org/core-stack-backend.git
cd core-stack-backend/installation
chmod +x install.sh
./install.sh

docs.title: Our Mission
docs.text:
To democratize geospatial intelligence and empower communities with the data and tools they need for sustainable resource planning, agricultural development, and participatory governance.

We believe that:

  1. Geospatial data should be accessible - Not locked behind paywalls or requiring specialized expertise
  2. Communities know best - Ground truth data from local stakeholders is invaluable
  3. Technology should serve people - Built for farmers, planners, and citizens, not just specialists
  4. Open collaboration wins - Community-built platforms outlast proprietary solutions

docs.title: Fragmented Landscapes
docs.text:
Current geospatial ecosystems suffer from:

  • Siloed Data - Different departments maintain separate, incompatible datasets
  • High Barriers - Commercial platforms are expensive; open-source tools require expertise
  • Limited Participation - End-users can't contribute ground truth or corrections
  • Vendor Lock-in - Proprietary formats and APIs create dependency
  • Academic vs Practical - Research tools don't meet operational needs

docs.title: The CoRE Stack Difference
docs.text: Problem Our Solution Expensive licenses 100% open source, free forever Cloud-only access Local-first execution mode Can't verify algorithms Full transparency, inspectable code Can't contribute data Integrated community engagement Complex setup 10-minute installation Poor rural connectivity Offline-capable with local computation

docs.title: Agriculture
docs.text:
Helping farmers and agricultural departments:

  • Farm Boundary Mapping - Precise field delineation using ML
  • Cropping Intensity Analysis - Multi-season cropping pattern detection
  • Site Suitability - Optimal crop and plantation recommendations
  • Irrigation Planning - Water resource assessment and allocation

docs.title: Water Resource Management
docs.text:
Supporting watershed planning and rejuvenation:

  • Micro-Watershed Delineation - Automated boundary generation
  • Runoff Calculation - Hydrological modeling for catchments
  • Surface Water Body Mapping - Pond and tank identification
  • Rejuvenation Planning - Strategic intervention recommendations

docs.title: Democratic Planning
docs.text:
Empowering local governance:

  • Gram Panchayat Integration - Village-level data and planning tools
  • Asset Mapping - NREGA and public work documentation
  • Conflict Visualization - Land use conflict identification
  • Participatory Data Collection - Community-driven ground truth

docs.title: Research & Policy
docs.text:
Enabling evidence-based decisions:

  • LULC Change Detection - Temporal analysis of land use transitions
  • Drought Assessment - Causality analysis and early warning
  • Terrain Classification - Slope, aspect, and geomorphometric analysis
  • Restoration Opportunity - Potential area identification

docs.title: 1. Local-First Computing
docs.text:
We pioneered a unique "Local Computation Mode" that allows users to:

  • Download pre-computed data from our public STAC catalog
  • Run algorithms locally without cloud credentials
  • Work offline in bandwidth-constrained environments
  • Scale from laptop to cloud as needs grow

docs.title: 2. Community Data Stewardship
docs.text:
Through our WhatsApp bot interface and community engagement features:

  • Farmers can validate and correct LULC classifications
  • Local experts can contribute traditional knowledge
  • Citizens can report changes and anomalies
  • Data quality improves through crowdsourced validation

docs.title: 3. Algorithm Transparency
docs.text:
All our computational pipelines are open and inspectable:

  • See exactly how LULC classifications are generated
  • Verify hydrological calculations
  • Audit change detection algorithms
  • Contribute improvements to science modules

docs.title: 4. Integration-Ready
docs.text:
Designed to work with existing infrastructure:

  • GeoServer - Publish layers to standard OGC services
  • Google Earth Engine - Scale to planetary analysis when needed
  • ODK/KoboToolbox - Connect with mobile data collection
  • CFPT APIs - Integrate with CommonsTech Foundation services

docs.title: Documentation as Public Infrastructure
docs.text:
For CoRE Stack, documentation is part of the product, not an afterthought.

That means:

  • a user should be able to start from Swagger or simple curl
  • a researcher should be able to run a computation from python -c or Django shell
  • a maintainer should be able to trace the same workflow into Celery tasks and backend modules
  • people should not need prior Django fluency just to contribute usefully

We therefore aim to document the same system at multiple levels of depth, using MkDocs Material features deliberately:

  • tabs for different execution paths
  • admonitions for prerequisites and caveats
  • expandable details for advanced material
  • code-linked reference pages for builders

docs.title: Human-Led, Agent-Ready Stewardship
docs.text:
We also treat documentation maintenance as a governance problem.

Near term, CoRE Stack should use:

  • GitHub Actions to build docs and detect drift
  • human maintainers to review and decide required changes
  • agentic tooling to suggest patches and highlight likely stale pages

Only after the signals are stable should more of that process be delegated to agents directly.

docs.title: Current (v1.x)
docs.text:
• Core hydrological modeling
  • LULC generation (v2, v3, v4)
  • Public API with authentication
  • WhatsApp bot interface
  • Local computation mode (beta)

docs.title: Near-term (v2.x)
docs.text:
• Enhanced local-first capabilities
  • Mobile app for ground truth collection
  • Real-time collaboration features
  • Advanced visualization dashboard
  • Plugin system for custom pipelines

docs.title: Long-term (v3.x)
docs.text:
• AI-assisted ground truth validation
  • Federated learning for model improvement
  • Multi-language support
  • Global deployment guidance
  • Training and certification programs

docs.title: vs. OpenStreetMap (OSM)
docs.text:
OSM is fantastic for base mapping, but CoRE Stack adds:

  • Computational Pipelines - Not just maps, but analysis
  • Domain-Specific Tools - Agriculture, hydrology focused
  • Local Execution - Process data on your hardware
  • Structured Data - Beyond tags, relational models

docs.title: vs. Google Earth Engine (GEE)
docs.text:
GEE provides massive computational power, but CoRE Stack offers:

  • No Google Account Required - Local-first mode
  • Community Ownership - Open source, not vendor-controlled
  • Offline Capability - Work without connectivity
  • Simplified APIs - Purpose-built for common workflows

docs.title: vs. Commercial GIS (ArcGIS, etc.)
docs.text:
Commercial platforms have features, but CoRE Stack delivers:

  • Zero License Costs - Free for all users
  • Modifiable Source - Adapt to your needs
  • Community Support - Collaborative problem solving
  • Transparency - No black-box algorithms

docs.title: Join the Movement
docs.text:
CoRE Stack is built by and for communities. Whether you're a:

  • Developer - Extend the platform
  • Researcher - Use and verify our algorithms
  • Planner - Apply data to real-world challenges
  • Citizen - Contribute ground truth

There's a place for you in the CoRE Stack ecosystem.

Get Started Contribute

docs.title: Acknowledgments
docs.text:
CoRE Stack builds upon the shoulders of giants:

  • Google Earth Engine - For planetary-scale computation
  • Django - For robust web framework
  • PostgreSQL/PostGIS - For spatial database excellence
  • GDAL/OGR - For geospatial data abstraction
  • OpenStreetMap - For open mapping inspiration

And most importantly, the communities who contribute data, code, and expertise.

docs.title: API Documentation
docs.text:
This section documents the backend API surface that is actually wired in core-stack-backend.

Primary route source:

  • nrm_app/urls.py

Public interactive entry point:

  • https://geoserver.core-stack.org/swagger/

docs.title: API Surface Map
docs.text:
Surface What it is Source Auth-free geography discovery states, districts, blocks, proposed blocks geoadmin/api.py API-key public data admin lookups, MWS data, layer URLs, geometries public_api/api.py Compute submission SWB, LULC, hydrology, terrain, and related tasks computing/urls.py

The backend route map places these under /api/v1/ in nrm_app/urls.py. The examples below therefore use URLs like https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/....

docs.title: 1. Auth-Free Geography Discovery
docs.text:
curl https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_states/

curl https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/proposed_blocks/

These are implemented in geoadmin/api.py.

docs.title: 2. API-Key Public Data
docs.text:
The API-key routes use the X-API-Key header, as defined in utilities/auth_check_decorator.py.

curl \
  -H "X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY" \
  "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_generated_layer_urls/?state=karnataka&district=raichur&tehsil=devadurga"

docs.title: 3. Compute Submission
docs.text:
Many compute routes queue work and return an immediate success message rather than the final dataset.

curl -X POST \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"state":"karnataka","district":"raichur","block":"devadurga","start_year":2022,"end_year":2023}' \
  https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/generate_swb/

The SWB handler is in computing/api.py.

docs.title: API Authentication
docs.text: Authentication behavior is implemented in utilities/auth_check_decorator.py.

docs.title: Auth Modes in Code
docs.text: Mode Header or requirement Source Typical use in backend Auth_free none auth_check_decorator.py geography discovery routes API_key X-API-Key auth_check_decorator.py public data endpoints JWT Authorization: Bearer ... auth_check_decorator.py authenticated app routes

docs.title: Auth-Free Routes
docs.text:
The clearest auth-free routes today are the geography discovery endpoints in geoadmin/api.py:

  • GET /api/v1/get_states/
  • GET /api/v1/get_districts/<state_id>/
  • GET /api/v1/get_blocks/<district_id>/
  • GET /api/v1/proposed_blocks/

Example:

curl https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_states/

docs.title: API Key Routes
docs.text:
The public-data routes in public_api/api.py are decorated with auth_type="API_key".

Use:

curl \
  -H "X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY" \
  "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_admin_details_by_latlon/?latitude=15.91&longitude=76.53"

curl \
  -H "X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY" \
  "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_generated_layer_urls/?state=karnataka&district=raichur&tehsil=devadurga"

docs.title: JWT Routes
docs.text:
The decorator also supports JWT bearer tokens:

curl \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_JWT_TOKEN" \
  https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/some-jwt-protected-route/

The header parsing logic is in utilities/auth_check_decorator.py.

docs.title: Practical Guidance
docs.text:
• Use auth-free geography routes for first contact and UI bootstrapping.
  • Use X-API-Key for the public data APIs in public_api.
  • Use Swagger to confirm which routes are exposed in the deployment you are targeting.

docs.title: Computing API Endpoints
docs.text:
The computing surface is defined in computing/urls.py and implemented in computing/api.py.

Most of these handlers queue work and return a quick success response, while the heavy lifting happens asynchronously in pipeline modules.

Note

Many of these request bodies include gee_account_id. That value should be the primary key of a Django GEEAccount created from a valid Google Cloud service account, as documented in Google Earth Engine.

docs.title: Current Behavior
docs.text:
Many handlers call apply_async(...) on Celery tasks from computing/api.py, for example:

  • hydrology in generate_fortnightly_hydrology()
  • LULC in lulc_for_tehsil()
  • SWB in generate_swb()

Two useful concrete patterns are visible in current code:

Minimal queue triggerAPI plus retrying task wrapper computing/api.py
@api_view(["POST"])
@schema(None)
def generate_lcw(request):
    try:
        state = request.data.get("state").lower()
        district = request.data.get("district").lower()
        block = request.data.get("block").lower()
        gee_account_id = request.data.get("gee_account_id")
        generate_lcw_conflict_data.apply_async(
            args=[state, district, block, gee_account_id], queue="nrm"
        )
        return Response({"Success": "Successfully initiated"})
    except Exception as exc:
        return Response({"Exception": exc})
computing/api.py
@api_view(["POST"])
@schema(None)
def generate_facilities_proximity(request):
    try:
        state = request.data.get("state").lower()
        district = request.data.get("district").lower()
        block = request.data.get("block").lower()
        gee_account_id = request.data.get("gee_account_id")
        generate_facilities_proximity_task.apply_async(
            args=[state, district, block, gee_account_id], queue="nrm"
        )
        return Response({"Success": "Successfully initiated"})
    except Exception as exc:
        return Response({"Exception": exc})

The second pattern is paired with a retry-aware Celery wrapper in generate_facilities_proximity_task(), which is documented further in Run Internal Tasks.

Note

Compute route protection is mixed in the current backend code. Some handlers use api_security_check(...), while others are plain @api_view(["POST"]) functions. Use Swagger and deployment policy as the final source for what is callable in a given environment.

docs.title: What Happens After Submission
docs.text:
The common shape of the compute flow is:

  1. route declared in computing/urls.py
  2. request handled in computing/api.py
  3. task queued with Celery
  4. pipeline module runs
  5. results are pushed toward GeoServer or stored metadata

The publication helper layer is in computing/utils.py.

docs.title: Internal API Access
docs.text:
This page shows how to authenticate against CoRE Stack's internal app APIs with a bearer token and test them from curl or Postman.

Primary sources:

  • users/urls.py
  • users/views.py
  • utilities/auth_check_decorator.py

docs.title: 1. Get a JWT Access Token
docs.text:
The login endpoint is wired at POST /api/v1/auth/login/ in users/urls.py. The response is customized in LoginView.

cURLPostman 
curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/auth/login/" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "username": "your_username",
    "password": "your_password"
  }'
  1. Create a POST request to https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/auth/login/
  2. Set Body -> raw -> JSON
  3. Send:
{
  "username": "your_username",
  "password": "your_password"
}
  1. Save the access token for the Authorization header
  2. Save the refresh token for refresh or logout

Typical response fields from the current backend:

  • access
  • refresh
  • user

docs.title: 2. Call a JWT-Protected Internal API
docs.text:
Some internal compute routes use api_security_check(...) with the default JWT mode. One example is POST /api/v1/generate_mws_layer/ from computing/api.py.

Use the access token as:

Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN
cURLPostman 
curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/generate_mws_layer/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "state": "odisha",
    "district": "koraput",
    "block": "jaypur",
    "gee_account_id": 1
  }'
  1. Create a POST request to https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/generate_mws_layer/
  2. In Authorization, choose Bearer Token
  3. Paste the access token returned by login
  4. Set Body -> raw -> JSON
  5. Send the same payload shown in the curl example

Current response shape:

{
  "Success": "Successfully initiated"
}

docs.title: 3. Refresh and Logout
docs.text:
Routes:

  • refresh: users/urls.py
  • logout: users/urls.py

docs.title: Refresh
docs.text:
curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/auth/token/refresh/" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "refresh": "YOUR_REFRESH_TOKEN"
  }'

docs.title: Logout
docs.text:
curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/auth/logout/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "refresh_token": "YOUR_REFRESH_TOKEN"
  }'

docs.title: 4. Important Caveat
docs.text:
The current compute surface mixes:

  • JWT-protected routes via api_security_check(...)
  • plain DRF @api_view(["POST"]) handlers without that decorator

So for a given internal workflow, treat the code and Swagger as the source of truth. This page documents the JWT path because it is the clearest bearer-token flow in the backend today.

docs.title: Public API Endpoints
docs.text:
This page documents the public-facing endpoints that are already present in the backend codebase.

There are two useful groups:

  1. auth-free geography discovery
  2. API-key public data access

docs.title: 1. Auth-Free Geography Discovery
docs.text:
Routes:

  • geoadmin/urls.py
  • geoadmin/api.py
Route What it returns Source GET /api/v1/get_states/ all states with normalized names get_states() GET /api/v1/get_districts/<state_id>/ districts for one state get_districts() GET /api/v1/get_blocks/<district_id>/ blocks or tehsils for one district get_blocks() GET /api/v1/proposed_blocks/ activated or proposed locations proposed_blocks()

Examples:

curl https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_states/

curl https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_districts/29/

docs.title: 2. API-Key Public Data Access
docs.text:
Routes:

docs.title: Generated Layer URL Payloads
docs.text:
The response builder for generated layers is in public_api/views.py.

Each layer entry is assembled from backend models and includes:

  • layer_name
  • dataset_name
  • layer_type
  • layer_url
  • layer_version
  • style_url
  • gee_asset_path

Example:

curl \
  -H "X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY" \
  "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/get_generated_layer_urls/?state=karnataka&district=raichur&tehsil=devadurga"

docs.title: Geometry Routes
docs.text:
The geometry helpers ultimately call GeoServer-backed WFS endpoints from public_api/views.py.

That makes these routes especially useful for:

  • UI teams that need polygons quickly
  • researchers who want MWS or village geometry dumps
  • data users building custom download or visualization pipelines

docs.title: Roles and Organizations
docs.text:
This page documents the current access model for account creation, organizations, and project-level roles in the backend.

Primary sources:

  • users/models.py
  • users/views.py
  • users/serializers.py
  • users/permissions.py
  • organization/views.py
  • projects/views.py

docs.title: Access Layers
docs.text:
Layer How it exists in code What it can do today Django superuser built-in is_superuser flag Django admin access and full backend power Superadmin custom User.is_superadmin field manage organizations and elevated user operations across the app Organization Admin Django group membership such as Organization Admin manage users and projects inside one organization Project member UserProjectGroup assignment hold project-scoped roles like manager, app user, or analyst

Warning

superuser and superadmin are not the same thing in CoRE Stack. The backend checks both in several places, so bootstrap instructions should treat them separately.

docs.title: Choose the Workflow
docs.text:
Join or RegisterBootstrap the First AdminCreate Organizations and ProjectsAssign Organization or Project Roles

The registration endpoint is:

  • POST /api/v1/auth/register/

Current backend behavior from UserRegistrationSerializer:

  • if organization is omitted, the user is created without one
  • if organization is a UUID, the serializer tries to attach that existing organization
  • if organization is a string name and no match is found, the serializer currently creates a new organization and attaches the user to it

Existing organization example:

curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/auth/register/" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "username": "researcher1",
    "email": "researcher1@example.org",
    "password": "strong-password",
    "password_confirm": "strong-password",
    "organization": "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
  }'

New organization name example:

curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/auth/register/" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "username": "steward1",
    "email": "steward1@example.org",
    "password": "strong-password",
    "password_confirm": "strong-password",
    "organization": "Koraput Community Lab"
  }'

Note

That name-based organization creation is current serializer behavior, not a separate governance workflow. Treat the code as the source of truth until product policy is formalized more clearly.

The first elevated operator still needs shell access. This is the one place where the docs should be explicit rather than pretending everything starts from Swagger.

  1. Create a Django superuser:
python manage.py createsuperuser
  1. Mark that user as a CoRE Stack superadmin:
python manage.py shell

from users.models import User

user = User.objects.get(username="your_admin_username")
user.is_superadmin = True
user.save(update_fields=["is_superadmin"])

Once one superadmin exists, elevated user management can move into the API.

Organization routes are exposed from:

  • organization/urls.py

Project routes are exposed from:

  • projects/urls.py

Practical rules from the current code:

  • organization creation is restricted to superadmins or Django superusers
  • organization retrieval and update are limited by object-level checks in OrganizationViewSet
  • project creation requires authentication plus organization membership
  • superadmins can create a project for any organization
  • non-superadmin organization members create projects inside their own organization

Create an organization:

curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/organizations/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "name": "Odisha Watershed Cell",
    "description": "Regional planning and hydrology team"
  }'

Create a project:

curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/projects/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "name": "Koraput Watershed Planning",
    "description": "Pilot planning project",
    "organization": "ORGANIZATION_UUID",
    "app_type": "watershed"
  }'

The least-Django path for admins is to work from authenticated API calls:

  • GET /api/v1/groups/
  • PUT /api/v1/users/{user_id}/set_organization/
  • PUT /api/v1/users/{user_id}/set_group/
  • GET /api/v1/users/my_projects/
  • POST /api/v1/projects/{project_id}/users/

Get available groups:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/groups/"

Assign a user to an organization:

curl -X PUT "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/users/42/set_organization/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "organization_id": "ORGANIZATION_UUID"
  }'

Assign a global group:

curl -X PUT "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/users/42/set_group/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "group_id": 3
  }'

Assign a project-specific role:

curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/projects/7/users/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "user_id": 42,
    "group_id": 3
  }'

Note

UserProjectGroupViewSet requires the user and project to belong to the same organization. It will reject cross-organization assignments.

docs.title: Adding New Pipelines
docs.text:
This builder page is the short route into CoRE Stack pipeline development.

For the detailed pattern library, read Pipeline Integration Pattern.

docs.title: What New Contributors Should Understand First
docs.text:
Before writing a new pipeline, trace one existing vector-heavy page and one raster-heavy page:

  • Facilities Proximity
  • Catchment Area

That will show you the actual integration surfaces the stack already uses.

docs.title: The Minimum Builder Checklist
docs.text:
• define the ROI and required inputs
  • decide whether the first useful output is vector, raster, or mixed
  • implement the processing function first
  • expose it through shell, Celery, API, or some combination
  • reuse shared helpers for GEE paths, publication, and metadata
  • document the new pipeline under docs/pipelines/

docs.title: Shared Code Surfaces
docs.text:
Start here when wiring a new pipeline:

  • computing/api.py
  • computing/urls.py
  • computing/utils.py
  • utilities/gee_utils.py

docs.title: Reusable Entry-Point Templates
docs.text: Two existing backend patterns are worth copying instead of re-inventing.

docs.title: 1. Minimal DRF Trigger for Async Work
docs.text:
The LCW route in generate_lcw() is the smallest useful HTTP entry point for a background computation.

computing/api.py
@api_view(["POST"])
@schema(None)
def generate_lcw(request):
    try:
        state = request.data.get("state").lower()  # (1)!
        district = request.data.get("district").lower()
        block = request.data.get("block").lower()
        gee_account_id = request.data.get("gee_account_id")
        generate_lcw_conflict_data.apply_async(  # (2)!
            args=[state, district, block, gee_account_id], queue="nrm"
        )
        return Response({"Success": "Successfully initiated"})  # (3)!
    except Exception as exc:
        return Response({"Exception": exc})
  1. Normalize request fields immediately if downstream asset naming, path generation, or filters expect lowercase text.
  2. Keep the HTTP handler thin. The route should enqueue work, and not contain any other logic.
  3. Return fast so frontend and Postman users get a quick acknowledgement instead of waiting for the full pipeline.

Use this pattern when the task itself already contains the real processing lifecycle and you do not need special retry logic at the API layer.

docs.title: 2. Celery Retry Wrapper Around a Direct Function
docs.text:
Facilities proximity uses a stronger wrapper in generate_facilities_proximity_task().

computing/misc/facilities_proximity.py
@app.task(bind=True, max_retries=3, default_retry_delay=60)
def generate_facilities_proximity_task(self, state, district, block, gee_account_id):
    """Celery task wrapper for generate_facilities_proximity"""
    try:
        return generate_facilities_proximity(state, district, block, gee_account_id)  # (1)!
    except Exception as exc:
        logger.error(f"Celery task error: {exc}")  # (2)!
        try:
            raise self.retry(exc=exc)  # (3)!
        except self.MaxRetriesExceededError:
            logger.error(f"Max retries exceeded for {state}/{district}/{block}")
            return False
  1. Keep the science or data-processing function separately callable from shell and tests.
  2. Log failures at the retry boundary so operators can inspect queue behavior.
  3. Use a wrapper like this when the underlying work touches GEE, GeoServer, network storage, or any dependency that may fail transiently.

This pattern matters because it gives CoRE Stack multiple access layers to the same logic:

  • direct function call from python -c
  • manage.py shell experimentation
  • async queue execution in production
  • API-triggered workflows for frontend and Postman users

docs.title: Recommended Build Sequence
docs.text:
1. Prototype the data logic for implementation.
  2. Make it callable from Django shell.
  3. Add task or API integration.
  4. Add publication and metadata persistence.
  5. Add docs, including a pipeline page and builder notes.

docs.title: Run Internal Tasks
docs.text:
You do not need deep Django knowledge to execute backend computations in CoRE Stack.

This page shows the same ecosystem from multiple entry points:

  • JWT-backed internal API calls
  • direct Python execution after django.setup()
  • manage.py shell
  • Celery task submission

Primary sources:

  • computing/misc/facilities_proximity.py
  • computing/api.py
  • computing/urls.py

Note

The example gee_account_id=1 values on this page are placeholders. Before using these task entry points, create a real Django GEEAccount and copy its ID from the admin UI as documented in Google Earth Engine.

docs.title: Choose Your Entry Point
docs.text: If you are... Start with Why API tester, analyst, or UI developer Internal API Access closest to frontend or Postman behavior Python user or researcher direct function call fastest path to one-off execution Backend contributor manage.py shell easy inspection and iteration Operator or maintainer Celery task call matches async production behavior

docs.title: Example A: Facilities Proximity
docs.text: The backend module itself documents a direct python -c usage pattern in computing/misc/facilities_proximity.py.

docs.title: Direct Python Without Opening a Shell
docs.text:
python -cWhy use this? 
python -c "
import os
os.environ.setdefault('DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE', 'nrm_app.settings')
import django
django.setup()
from computing.misc.facilities_proximity import generate_facilities_proximity
generate_facilities_proximity('Odisha', 'Koraput', 'Jaypur', gee_account_id=1)
"
  • good for one-off experiments
  • easy to paste into docs, issues, or chat
  • avoids needing to understand Django management commands first

docs.title: Same Logic from manage.py shell
docs.text:
python manage.py shell

from computing.misc.facilities_proximity import generate_facilities_proximity

generate_facilities_proximity("Odisha", "Koraput", "Jaypur", gee_account_id=1)

The direct function is implemented in generate_facilities_proximity().

Replace 1 with the actual gee_account_id from your environment.

docs.title: Async Execution Through Celery
docs.text:
python manage.py shell

from computing.misc.facilities_proximity import generate_facilities_proximity_task

generate_facilities_proximity_task.delay("Odisha", "Koraput", "Jaypur", 1)

The Celery wrapper is implemented in generate_facilities_proximity_task().

docs.title: Why This Task Pattern Matters
docs.text:
The facilities proximity wrapper is a good example of a production-ready task boundary because it keeps the callable science function separate from retry behavior.

computing/misc/facilities_proximity.py
@app.task(bind=True, max_retries=3, default_retry_delay=60)
def generate_facilities_proximity_task(self, state, district, block, gee_account_id):
    """Celery task wrapper for generate_facilities_proximity"""
    try:
        return generate_facilities_proximity(state, district, block, gee_account_id)  # (1)!
    except Exception as exc:
        logger.error(f"Celery task error: {exc}")  # (2)!
        try:
            raise self.retry(exc=exc)  # (3)!
        except self.MaxRetriesExceededError:
            logger.error(f"Max retries exceeded for {state}/{district}/{block}")
            return False
  1. The core function stays usable from python -c and manage.py shell.
  2. Queue failures become visible to maintainers.
  3. Retries are added without duplicating the processing logic.

docs.title: Example B: Internal API Route
docs.text:
Facilities proximity is also exposed as an internal API route:

  • route: computing/urls.py
  • handler: generate_facilities_proximity()
curl -X POST "https://geoserver.core-stack.org/api/v1/generate_facilities_proximity/" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "state": "odisha",
    "district": "koraput",
    "block": "jaypur",
    "gee_account_id": 1
  }'

Current response:

{
  "Success": "Successfully initiated"
}

Note

This specific route is currently a plain @api_view(["POST"]) handler, not a JWT-decorated route. If you need a bearer-token example, use the JWT flow documented in Internal API Access.

docs.title: Another API Pattern: Minimal Async Trigger
docs.text:
LCW conflict uses a very thin route in generate_lcw(), which is useful when you want the API to validate inputs lightly, queue work, and return immediately.

computing/api.py
@api_view(["POST"])
@schema(None)
def generate_lcw(request):
    try:
        state = request.data.get("state").lower()  # (1)!
        district = request.data.get("district").lower()
        block = request.data.get("block").lower()
        gee_account_id = request.data.get("gee_account_id")
        generate_lcw_conflict_data.apply_async(  # (2)!
            args=[state, district, block, gee_account_id], queue="nrm"
        )
        return Response({"Success": "Successfully initiated"})  # (3)!
    except Exception as exc:
        return Response({"Exception": exc})
  1. Input normalization is done before task submission.
  2. The view acts as a queue trigger, not the place where clipping and publication happen.
  3. This is the right pattern when HTTP clients need a fast acknowledgement.

That task is then executed in computing/misc/lcw_conflict.py, where the real pan-India clip workflow runs.

docs.title: Which Path Should You Prefer?
docs.text:
Beginner or non-Django contributorResearcher or analystBackend maintainer

Start with Swagger, curl, or Postman. You can contribute examples, docs, and QA without first learning Django internals.

Use python -c or manage.py shell for reproducible experiments and fast validation against real backend code.

Use Celery task entry points when you want production-like behavior, retries, and queue-level observability.

docs.title: Documentation Principle
docs.text:
For public-interest infrastructure like CoRE Stack, "how do I run this?" must be answered at multiple levels. A contributor should be able to start from whichever layer feels natural:

  • browser
  • curl
  • Postman
  • python -c
  • Django shell
  • Celery task

That is a documentation requirement, not optional polish.

docs.title: System Architecture
docs.text: This page explains the current backend architecture as implemented in core-stack-backend, with links into the actual source files.

docs.title: Request Routing
docs.text:
graph TB
    A[Swagger or Client] --> B[nrm_app/urls.py]
    B --> C[geoadmin]
    B --> D[public_api]
    B --> E[computing]
    E --> F[Celery-backed pipeline modules]
    D --> G[GeoServer and DB-backed metadata]
    F --> H[GEE utilities]
    F --> I[GeoServer publishing helpers]
    C --> J[State, district, block models]
    D --> J

The top-level Django router is nrm_app/urls.py. It wires:

  • auth-free geographic discovery routes from geoadmin/urls.py
  • API-key public-data routes from public_api/urls.py
  • compute submission routes from computing/urls.py
  • Swagger and ReDoc from nrm_app/urls.py

docs.title: Runtime Layers
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Django as Django API handler
    participant Celery as Celery task queue
    participant Pipeline as pipeline module
    participant GEE as GEE or GCS helpers
    participant GeoServer as GeoServer publishing

    Client->>Django: request
    Django->>Celery: apply_async(...)
    Celery->>Pipeline: run compute pipeline
    Pipeline->>GEE: read or export assets when needed
    Pipeline->>GeoServer: publish output layer
    Django-->>Client: immediate success response

docs.title: geoadmin
docs.text:
• Implements auth-free location discovery in geoadmin/api.py
  • Backs state, district, and block lookup flows used before heavier computation

docs.title: public_api
docs.text:
• Exposes public-data lookups in public_api/api.py
  • Builds layer download metadata in public_api/views.py
  • Fetches MWS and village geometries from GeoServer in public_api/views.py

docs.title: computing
docs.text:
• Declares the backend compute surface in computing/urls.py
  • Queues SWB, LULC, hydrology, terrain, and other tasks from computing/api.py
  • Publishes vector and raster outputs toward GeoServer via computing/utils.py

docs.title: utilities
docs.text:
• Handles auth mode parsing and headers in utilities/auth_check_decorator.py
  • Handles Earth Engine initialization, task polling, and asset path helpers in utilities/gee_utils.py

Note

In operational terms, this means most compute deployments need a configured Django GEEAccount before pipeline execution will work reliably. The setup flow is documented in Google Earth Engine.

# computing/api.py
from computing.my_pipeline.my_pipeline import run_my_analysis

@api_view(["POST"])
def my_pipeline(request):
    """API endpoint for my pipeline."""
    state = request.data.get("state")
    district = request.data.get("district")
    block = request.data.get("block")

    result = run_my_analysis(state, district, block)

docs.title: Contribution Showcase
docs.text:
This page tracks major community-built analyses, datasets, dashboards, and tools that extend the CoRE Stack ecosystem. The focus here is on work that can inform or integrate with core repositories such as core-stack-backend, landscape-explorer, and related CoRE Stack applications.

We want to highlight substantive contributions such as reusable datasets, pipeline ideas, validation tools, dashboards, and integrations built through open-source collaboration, innovation challenges, and follow-on community work.

docs.title: Innovation Challenge Contributions
docs.text: Contributor Contribution Current artifact Why it matters for CoRE Stack Current integration direction Sanket Gharat Nashik onion dynamics dashboard Live dashboard combines cropping intensity, waterbody layers, onion price signals, and mandi proximity into a district-level analytical workflow reusable agri-market analytics pattern for district dashboards and backend-backed analyses Anoop Asranna Anekal biodiversity dashboard Live dashboard augments waterbody records with biodiversity observations from eBird and iNaturalist pathway toward biodiversity-aware lake and waterbody views in CoRE Stack surfaces Trishal Kumar Fields / Field Validator GitHub repository enables offline-first field validation of land-cover layers using CoRE Stack APIs and Dynamic World supports ground-truth collection and calibration workflows for future layer and pipeline improvement

docs.title: Sanket Gharat
docs.text:
Sanket's Nashik work shows how CoRE Stack layers can be combined with external market and infrastructure data to ask practical planning questions. The project studies Nashik district from 2017 to 2025, relating onion prices to cropping intensity, land-use transitions, waterbodies, and distance from mandis and traders.

  • A dashboard combining onion price time series, mapped market actors, cropping-intensity layers, and remote-sensing-derived waterbodies.
  • The work is especially valuable because it turns CoRE Stack data into an econometric and decision-support workflow rather than only a visualization.
  • This contribution can help in integrating agri-market related analysis to CoRE Stack backend.

docs.title: Anoop Asranna
docs.text:
Anoop's Anekal biodiversity dashboard connects lake monitoring with ecological observation data. it shows a workflow for waterbodies in Anekal that merges CoRE Stack lake metadata, OpenStreetMap geometries, and citizen-science observations from eBird and iNaturalist.

  • The project includes historical biodiversity records, buffered lake matching, and a repeatable update workflow for 2026 observations.
  • Integration with waterbody dashboards lets us analyse biodiversity change alongside land-use change and climate-related indicators.
  • Such enrichment contribution can materially deepen landscape-explorer and other waterbody-facing interfaces.

docs.title: Trishal Kumar
docs.text:
Trishal's Fields project is a mobile-first field validation application for land-use and land-cover datasets. It is designed to work offline in the field while still integrating with CoRE Stack APIs, Google Earth Engine Dynamic World layers, and exported observation workflows.

  • The app supports GPS-based field observations, photographs, offline caching, later sync, and JSON or CSV export.
  • CoRE Stack layers are generated based on real ground-truth observations and need local calibration, making this project highly relevant to future validation loops around pipeline outputs.

docs.title: How We Will Maintain This Page
docs.text:
We will keep adding major ecosystem contributions here as they come in. Priority goes to work that has at least one of the following:

  • a live demo, public repository, or reproducible notebook
  • a clear relationship to CoRE Stack datasets, APIs, explorer surfaces, or backend integrations
  • evidence that the work adds new data, validation capacity, analytical value, or reusable tooling

docs.title: CoRE Stack Innovation Challenge
docs.text:
The CoRE Stack Innovation Challenge launched on November 22, 2025. As of March 11, 2026, it is being run as a rolling contribution program rather than a fixed-deadline contest.

The challenge invites ecologists, hydrologists, geospatial programmers, students, NGOs, and community technologists to build open-source analyses, tools, and integrations using CoRE Stack datasets and APIs.

docs.text:
• Data exploration and insights: reproducible analyses, maps, and indicators built from CoRE Stack layers
  • Tooling, APIs, and developer tools: wrappers, libraries, utilities, and workflow helpers
  • Data stories and UX integrations: dashboards, chat interfaces, practitioner tools, and communication-oriented products

docs.title: Innovation Challenges
docs.text: CoRE Stack is currently supporting two open innovation tracks for students, researchers, and builders who want to work with geospatial data, remote sensing, and public-interest tooling.

docs.title: Current Calls
docs.text:
• TESSERA Student Innovation Call: a rolling call supported by the University of Cambridge for projects built around TESSERA Earth observation embeddings.
  • CoRE Stack Innovation Challenge: the broader CoRE Stack challenge for open-source analyses, tools, data stories, and integrations.

docs.title: How the Two Calls Relate
docs.text:
TESSERA is a model-centered student research call. The CoRE Stack Innovation Challenge is the broader application and integration pathway.

If your idea depends on Earth observation embeddings, start with TESSERA. If your idea focuses on CoRE Stack datasets, APIs, visualizations, or reusable tooling, start with the CoRE Stack challenge. Projects can span both tracks when the work fits both sets of goals.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
• Read the TESSERA call
  • Read the CoRE Stack challenge details

docs.title: TESSERA Student Innovation Call
docs.text: This rolling call is supported by the University of Cambridge and centers on TESSERA, an Earth observation foundation model that produces 10 m global embeddings from satellite imagery.

docs.title: What Is TESSERA?
docs.text: TESSERA processes satellite imagery at global scale and produces compact embeddings that capture land cover, vegetation, forest structure, and environmental change over time. These embeddings can be used directly in downstream tasks without requiring participants to build the full remote-sensing preprocessing stack themselves.

docs.title: What Participants Can Work On
docs.text:
• forest monitoring and deforestation detection
  • agricultural mapping and crop classification
  • urban expansion and land-use change analysis
  • disaster-response use cases such as flood or wildfire mapping
  • biodiversity, habitat, carbon, and climate-risk analysis
  • infrastructure monitoring in remote regions

docs.title: What the Call Provides
docs.text:
• access to pre-computed TESSERA embeddings for the region and time period of interest
  • technical guidance from the Cambridge TESSERA research team
  • recognition through the open research community, with strong projects potentially leading to broader collaboration
  • a path to submit eligible work into the CoRE Stack Innovation Challenge

docs.title: Path 2: Install the Backend
docs.text:
The backend already ships with a deployment-oriented installer in installation/install.sh. The backend repo also documents required manual prerequisites in installation/INSTALLATION.md.

Linux (Ubuntu or Debian)Windows (WSL2)

The smoothest path.

git clone https://github.com/core-stack-org/core-stack-backend.git
cd core-stack-backend/installation
chmod +x install.sh
./install.sh

Detailed Installer Guide

Run the backend inside WSL2. If your checkout is on Y:\core-stack-org\core-stack-backend, that path appears inside WSL as /mnt/y/core-stack-org/core-stack-backend.

wsl --install

cd /mnt/y/core-stack-org/core-stack-backend/installation
chmod +x install.sh
./install.sh

Detailed Installer Guide

docs.title: What the Installer Automates
docs.text:
Based on installation/install.sh, the current script automates:

  1. Miniconda Installation - Python environment management
  2. Conda Environment Creation - from environment.yml
  3. PostgreSQL Setup - database and user creation
  4. Apache Configuration - mod_wsgi deployment
  5. Backend Clone or Deploy Setup - /var/www/data/corestack by default
  6. .env Generation - from settings references
  7. Django Migrations and Static Files

The backend install guide currently documents additional manual prerequisites and follow-up, including RabbitMQ and environment credential setup.

conda activate corestack-backend
cd /var/www/data/corestack
python manage.py check
python manage.py runserver

  • Admin: http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/

docs.text: After installation, use this page to confirm that the backend is alive, that background workers can start, and that you can hit the real API surface.

docs.title: 1. Start Django

conda activate corestack-backend
cd /var/www/data/corestack
python manage.py runserver 127.0.0.1:8000

Open:

  • http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/
  • http://127.0.0.1:8000/swagger/

Route source:

  • nrm_app/urls.py

docs.title: 2. Start Celery
docs.text:
conda activate corestack-backend
cd /var/www/data/corestack
celery -A nrm_app worker -l info -Q nrm

This is necessary for the task-submission compute endpoints in computing/api.py.

docs.title: 3. Test an Auth-Free Route
docs.text:
curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/v1/get_states/

curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/v1/proposed_blocks/

These routes come from geoadmin/api.py.

docs.title: 4. Test an API-Key Route
docs.text:
The public-data routes use the X-API-Key header from utilities/auth_check_decorator.py.

curl \
  -H "X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY" \
  "http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/v1/get_admin_details_by_latlon/?latitude=15.91&longitude=76.53"

curl \
  -H "X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY" \
  "http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/v1/get_generated_layer_urls/?state=karnataka&district=raichur&tehsil=devadurga"

docs.title: 5. Submit a Compute Task
docs.text:
Example using SWB:

curl -X POST \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"state":"karnataka","district":"raichur","block":"devadurga","start_year":2022,"end_year":2023}' \
  http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/v1/generate_swb/

Implementation:

  • generate_swb()

Expected behavior:

  • the API responds quickly
  • the Celery worker logs the background job
  • final output publication happens asynchronously

docs.title: 6. Verify Admin Data Exists
docs.text:
If the auth-free geography routes return empty data, inspect the admin tables or populate them:

python manage.py populate_state_soi
python manage.py populate_district_soi
python manage.py populate_tehsil_soi

docs.title: Interactive Installation Guide
docs.text: This page turns the current backend installer into a reliable walkthrough. It is based on the actual script in installation/install.sh and the backend install notes in installation/INSTALLATION.md.

docs.title: Phase 1: Manual Prerequisites
docs.text: The current backend install guide explicitly separates manual prerequisites from automated installation.

docs.title: Operating System
docs.text:
• Linux is the primary target
  • Windows users should use WSL2
  • if your backend checkout is on Y:\core-stack-org\core-stack-backend, use /mnt/y/core-stack-org/core-stack-backend inside WSL

docs.title: Required Packages
docs.text:
Run this first:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y git wget curl build-essential libpq-dev rabbitmq-server
sudo systemctl enable rabbitmq-server
sudo systemctl start rabbitmq-server

Note

RabbitMQ is called out in the backend installation guide as a manual prerequisite for Celery-backed task processing.

docs.title: Phase 2: Review the Installer Configuration
docs.text:
The installer is parameterized near the top of install.sh.

Defaults worth knowing:

Variable Default MINICONDA_DIR $HOME/miniconda3 CONDA_ENV_NAME corestack-backend BACKEND_DIR /var/www/data/corestack POSTGRES_USER nrm POSTGRES_DB nrm POSTGRES_PASSWORD nrm@123

If those are acceptable, you can run the installer as-is.

docs.title: Phase 3: Run the Installer
docs.text:
Using the existing local checkoutCloning fresh 
cd /mnt/y/core-stack-org/core-stack-backend/installation
chmod +x install.sh
./install.sh

git clone https://github.com/core-stack-org/core-stack-backend.git
cd core-stack-backend/installation
chmod +x install.sh
./install.sh

What the script currently does, based on the functions defined in install.sh:

  • installs Miniconda
  • creates the corestack-backend conda environment
  • installs PostgreSQL and creates the configured database and user
  • installs Apache and mod_wsgi
  • clones or updates the backend into /var/www/data/corestack
  • creates the logs directory
  • generates nrm_app/.env
  • runs collectstatic
  • runs migrations
  • writes Apache site config and reloads Apache

docs.title: Phase 4: Manual Follow-Up
docs.text: The installer does not finish everything needed for a real working deployment.

docs.title: Review the generated .env
docs.text:
The script generates:

/var/www/data/corestack/nrm_app/.env

Edit it and supply the real credentials and secrets your environment needs.

At minimum, check:

  • SECRET_KEY
  • DEBUG
  • GeoServer credentials
  • Google Earth Engine related credentials or account wiring
  • email or integration credentials if your workflow uses them

docs.title: Create an admin user
docs.text:
source "$HOME/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh"
conda activate corestack-backend
cd /var/www/data/corestack
python manage.py createsuperuser

docs.title: Start a development check
docs.text:
python manage.py check
python manage.py runserver 127.0.0.1:8000

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Use these quick checks:

curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/v1/get_states/

Open:

  • http://127.0.0.1:8000/swagger/
  • http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/

If compute jobs are part of your test, also start a Celery worker:

celery -A nrm_app worker -l info -Q nrm

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: Common issues when installing, starting, or testing the current CoRE Stack backend.

docs.title: conda: command not found
docs.text:
The installer expects Miniconda at the configured location in installation/install.sh.

Try:

source "$HOME/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh"
conda activate corestack-backend

docs.title: PostgreSQL connection errors
docs.text:
sudo systemctl status postgresql
sudo systemctl start postgresql
sudo systemctl enable postgresql

Check that the configured database and user from install.sh exist.

docs.title: RabbitMQ is missing
docs.text:
Celery-backed compute flows need RabbitMQ according to the backend installation guide.

sudo apt install -y rabbitmq-server
sudo systemctl start rabbitmq-server
sudo systemctl enable rabbitmq-server

docs.title: Django Starts, But the API Looks Wrong
Swagger is not loading
docs.text:
Confirm the route is wired in nrm_app/urls.py, then verify the server is actually running:

python manage.py runserver 127.0.0.1:8000

Open:

  • http://127.0.0.1:8000/swagger/

docs.title: You are calling stale route names
docs.text:
The current auth-free geography routes are:

  • GET /api/v1/get_states/
  • GET /api/v1/get_districts/<state_id>/
  • GET /api/v1/get_blocks/<district_id>/
  • GET /api/v1/proposed_blocks/

They are implemented in geoadmin/api.py.

docs.title: 401 Unauthorized on public-data APIs
docs.text:
The public_api routes use X-API-Key, not Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY.

Source:

  • utilities/auth_check_decorator.py

Use:

curl \
  -H "X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY" \
  "http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/v1/get_generated_layer_urls/?state=karnataka&district=raichur&tehsil=devadurga"

docs.title: JWT route confusion
docs.text:
If a route expects JWT, the decorator checks:

Authorization: Bearer YOUR_JWT_TOKEN

Source:

  • utilities/auth_check_decorator.py

docs.title: Worker will not start
docs.text:
conda activate corestack-backend
cd /var/www/data/corestack
python manage.py check
celery -A nrm_app worker -l info -Q nrm

docs.title: Compute call returns success, but no final layer appears
docs.text:
Many compute handlers only submit background work. Check:

  1. the Celery worker logs
  2. GeoServer connectivity
  3. any environment credentials needed by that pipeline

Relevant code:

  • computing/api.py
  • computing/utils.py
  • utilities/gee_utils.py

docs.title: States or blocks are empty
docs.text:
Populate the geographic tables:

python manage.py populate_state_soi
python manage.py populate_district_soi
python manage.py populate_tehsil_soi

docs.title: Generated layer URLs return nothing
docs.text:
That route depends on stored layer metadata and GeoServer-aware dataset records in public_api/views.py. If it returns empty or 404:

  • check that the location exists
  • check that layers were actually generated
  • check that metadata exists in the backend database

docs.title: Google Earth Engine
docs.text:
Google Earth Engine is not an optional edge integration in the current CoRE Stack backend. Most computing pipelines, many local API-triggered runs, and several publication flows depend on a valid backend-side GEE account.

If you see gee_account_id in pipeline docs, API examples, or direct Python calls, it refers to a Django GEEAccount record created from a Google Cloud service account that has Earth Engine access.

Warning

For the current backend, you should assume GEE setup is required before trying to run most computing pipelines end-to-end. Without it, many local and queued compute paths will fail during ee_initialize(...).

docs.title: What You Need Before Running Pipelines
docs.text:
Set up these pieces in order:

  1. a Google Cloud project with Earth Engine enabled
  2. a service account with the required permissions
  3. a downloaded JSON key for that service account
  4. a Django admin GEEAccount entry
  5. the resulting gee_account_id for API calls, Celery tasks, and shell usage

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Google Cloud for Earth Engine
docs.text:
1. Go to the Google Cloud Earth Engine configuration page: https://console.cloud.google.com/earth-engine/configuration
  2. Create a new Google Cloud project, or select an existing one.
  1. Register that project for Earth Engine use.
  1. Review IAM permissions and make sure the account you are using can manage the project and service accounts.
  1. Create a service account in that project.
  1. Create a JSON key for that service account and download it.

The downloaded file will look something like <project-name>-12345-356644b54.json.

Danger

Save the JSON key securely. It grants access to your Google Cloud resources. Do not commit it to git, do not place it under docs/, and do not share it in issues or chat logs.

docs.title: Step 2: Add the Service Account to Django
Option A: Through Django Admin
docs.text:
For the current backend, this is the operator-facing path that lines up with how utilities/gee_utils.py loads credentials from the database.

  1. Open the Django admin add form for GEE accounts: http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/gee_computing/geeaccount/add/
  2. Fill in:
  3. Name: a recognizable label such as production or geo_org_gee
  4. Service Account Email: the email from the JSON key, such as name@project-id.iam.gserviceaccount.com
  5. Helper Account: leave blank unless you already know you need to chain to another stored account
  6. Is Visible: usually enabled
  7. Credentials File: upload the downloaded service account JSON key

  1. Save the record.
  2. After creation, copy the numeric account ID from the URL.
  3. Example URL: http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/gee_computing/geeaccount/21/change/
  4. In this case, the gee_account_id is 21

Use that gee_account_id in compute API requests, direct function calls, and Celery task submissions.

docs.title: Option B: Scripted or Shell-Based Setup
docs.text:
If you prefer to create the encrypted GEEAccount record from the backend repo instead of using the admin UI, use the lower-level guide here:

  • GEE Authentication Setup

That page covers test_gee_auth.py, manual Django-shell creation, .env defaults, and troubleshooting.

docs.title: Step 3: Use gee_account_id When Running Pipelines
docs.text:
Once the Django record exists, the ID becomes part of normal compute execution.

Example API body:

{
  "state": "karnataka",
  "district": "raichur",
  "block": "devadurga",
  "gee_account_id": 21
}

Example direct Python usage:

generate_facilities_proximity("Odisha", "Koraput", "Jaypur", gee_account_id=21)

You will see this parameter throughout the docs because it is how many handlers pass the GEE credential context into Celery-backed pipeline code.

Note

Some backend paths may fall back to GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID, but you should still create and verify a real GEEAccount first. Treat the admin-created account as part of baseline environment setup for computing work.

docs.title: Where GEE Is Wired in the Backend
docs.text:
The main helper module is:

  • utilities/gee_utils.py

Key responsibilities there:

  • initialize Earth Engine from stored credentials
  • initialize Google Cloud Storage access
  • validate and build asset paths
  • poll task status
  • create asset folders

Many compute handlers accept a gee_account_id parameter and pass it into Celery-backed pipeline tasks.

Examples:

  • hydrology handlers in computing/api.py
  • LULC handlers in computing/api.py
  • SWB handler in computing/api.py

The public API also uses Earth Engine for spatial lookups:

  • admin lookup by coordinates in public_api/views.py
  • report and MWS lookup helpers in public_api/views.py

docs.title: Geospatial Processing Pipelines
docs.text:
This section documents the pipeline surfaces that already exist in CoRE Stack and the reusable patterns behind them.

Use it for two different jobs:

  • understanding what current pipelines produce
  • learning how new pipelines become fully integrated into the stack

Primary backend surfaces:

  • computing/urls.py
  • computing/api.py
  • computing/utils.py
  • utilities/gee_utils.py

Note

For the current backend, most end-to-end computing pipelines assume Google Earth Engine is already configured. If you do not yet have a valid Django GEEAccount and gee_account_id, start with Google Earth Engine before trying the pipeline pages below.

docs.title: Read This Section in Order
docs.text:
1. Start with this overview page.
  2. Read Pipeline Integration Pattern to understand the shared building blocks.
  3. Jump into the pipeline family you care about.
  4. If you want to prototype before wiring the full publication stack, continue into Builder Guide: Adding Pipelines.

docs.title: The Common Integration Pattern
docs.text:
Most fully integrated pipelines in CoRE Stack follow the same high-level shape, even when the science changes.

flowchart LR
    A[curl, Postman, Swagger,<br/>Django shell, or Celery] --> B[computing/api.py or direct task]
    B --> C[Pipeline module]
    C --> D[Earth Engine or local processing]
    D --> E[Layer metadata in DB]
    D --> F[GeoServer publication]
    D --> G[STAC or auxiliary outputs]

Typical reusable components:

Concern Common helpers or files Why builders care naming and asset paths valid_gee_text(), get_gee_asset_path(), get_gee_dir_path() in utilities/gee_utils.py keep layer names, asset IDs, and folder paths consistent auth and GEE initialization ee_initialize() in utilities/gee_utils.py most GEE-backed tasks start here vector export export_vector_asset_to_gee(), upload_shp_to_gee() in utilities/gee_utils.py standard vector publication path raster export sync_raster_to_gcs() and sync_raster_gcs_to_geoserver() in utilities/gee_utils.py standard raster publication path GeoServer sync sync_fc_to_geoserver(), sync_layer_to_geoserver(), push_shape_to_geoserver() in computing/utils.py turns outputs into map-ready layers metadata tracking save_layer_info_to_db() and sync status helpers in computing/utils.py keeps outputs discoverable by the rest of the platform route exposure computing/api.py and computing/urls.py makes the pipeline callable from API clients

docs.title: Core Analytical Workflows
docs.text: Pipeline Primary outputs Best entry point LULC Generation classified raster products and downstream land-use layers land-cover and classification workflows Surface Water Body Detection vector waterbody outputs and derived joins waterbody mapping and staged execution study Hydrology runoff, recharge, watershed statistics, related layers hydrology and watershed planning Terrain Analysis slope, aspect, hillshade, terrain descriptors DEM-driven terrain workflows

docs.text: Pipeline Pattern Page administrative boundary extraction vector clip, publish, and metadata sync Admin Boundary facilities and service access enrichment tabular join plus geometry publication Facilities Proximity NREGA work sites vector clip from external data NREGA Assets agroecological zones pan-India vector clip Agroecological Space aquifer characterization vector intersection and weighted attributes Aquifer Vector groundwater extraction stage vector intersection and dominant-class assignment SOGE Vector restoration planning raster plus vector summary outputs Restoration Opportunity shared vector clip family same pattern applied to several external datasets Pan-India Clip Modules

docs.text: Pipeline or support surface What it helps with Page NDVI time series temporal vegetation analysis by class NDVI Time Series HLS interpolated NDVI gap-filled vegetation time-series support functions HLS Interpolated NDVI GEE operator setup Google Cloud, Earth Engine registration, Django admin account creation, and gee_account_id lookup Google Earth Engine GEE troubleshooting scripted setup, manual Django-shell setup, and failure recovery GEE Authentication Setup

docs.title: What Builders Should Learn From These Pages
docs.text:
The goal is not to memorize every pipeline.

The goal is to recognize the repeated components you can reuse when building a new one:

  • how ROI and asset names are constructed
  • how a pipeline decides whether it produces vector, raster, or mixed outputs
  • how results are published to GeoServer and saved to the database
  • how task entry points are exposed through API routes, shell, or Celery

If you are building something new, read Pipeline Integration Pattern next, then compare one vector-heavy page such as Facilities Proximity and one raster-heavy page such as Catchment Area.

docs.title: Incremental Prototyping
docs.text:
Not every pipeline needs to be your first execution target.

If you are new and do not yet have GEE, GeoServer, or AWS wired:

  • use these pages to understand the integrated architecture
  • use Builder Guide: Adding Pipelines to stage work from direct execution toward full integration
  • start with public APIs, docs, and local experimentation before trying to publish new layers

docs.title: Pipeline Integration Pattern
docs.text:
This page explains how existing CoRE Stack pipelines become fully integrated into the stack.

Read it as a pattern library, not as a single algorithm description.

Primary code surfaces:

  • computing/api.py
  • computing/urls.py
  • computing/utils.py
  • utilities/gee_utils.py

docs.title: What "Fully Integrated" Means Here
docs.text:
An integrated pipeline usually does most of the following:

  • accepts a stable ROI such as state, district, block, or project ROI
  • exposes an entry point through API, Django shell, or Celery
  • uses shared naming and asset-path helpers
  • runs the actual processing step
  • saves resulting layer metadata to the database
  • publishes vector or raster outputs to GeoServer when needed
  • optionally creates STAC or related catalog metadata
  • gets documented in this docs site
flowchart LR
    A[Route or shell trigger] --> B[Task or direct function]
    B --> C[Processing logic]
    C --> D[Asset creation or local output]
    D --> E[Metadata in DB]
    D --> F[GeoServer]
    D --> G[STAC or related specs]

docs.title: Reusable Building Blocks
docs.text:
Concern Typical helpers Where to look GEE setup ee_initialize() utilities/gee_utils.py naming and path normalization valid_gee_text(), get_gee_asset_path(), get_gee_dir_path() utilities/gee_utils.py vector export to GEE export_vector_asset_to_gee(), upload_shp_to_gee() utilities/gee_utils.py raster export and publication sync_raster_to_gcs(), sync_raster_gcs_to_geoserver() utilities/gee_utils.py vector publication sync_fc_to_geoserver(), sync_layer_to_geoserver(), push_shape_to_geoserver() computing/utils.py layer bookkeeping save_layer_info_to_db() and sync status helpers computing/utils.py route exposure DRF views and task triggers computing/api.py, computing/urls.py

Note

If your pipeline uses ee_initialize() or accepts gee_account_id, document the setup prerequisite explicitly and point operators to Google Earth Engine. In the current stack, that setup is required for most real compute runs.

docs.title: 1. Vector Clip and Publish
docs.text:
Used by pages such as:

  • Admin Boundary
  • Drainage Lines
  • NREGA Assets
  • Agroecological Space

Typical pattern:

  1. build or load ROI
  2. filter an external vector dataset
  3. export to GEE or write local shapes
  4. save layer metadata
  5. publish to GeoServer

docs.title: 2. Raster Clip and Publish
docs.text:
Used by pages such as:

  • Catchment Area
  • Distance to Nearest Drainage
  • Natural Depression
  • Slope Percentage

Typical pattern:

  1. build ROI
  2. load a source raster
  3. clip or derive the raster
  4. export raster to GEE or GCS
  5. publish through GeoServer

docs.title: 3. Vector Enrichment or Spatial Join
docs.text:
Used by pages such as:

  • Facilities Proximity
  • Aquifer Vector
  • SOGE Vector

Typical pattern:

  1. load ROI or administrative geometry
  2. join with an external table or vector layer
  3. compute attributes or dominant classes
  4. export and publish the resulting feature collection

docs.title: 4. Mixed Raster and Vector Outputs
docs.text:
Used by pages such as:

  • Stream Order
  • Restoration Opportunity
  • Surface Water Body Detection

These pages are useful when you want to study both raster publication and vector summary logic in one place.

docs.title: 5. Time-Series Helpers
docs.text:
Used by pages such as:

  • NDVI Time Series
  • HLS Interpolated NDVI

These are useful when your new pipeline depends on temporal compositing, per-class aggregation, or derived vegetation signals.

docs.title: Step 1: Decide the smallest useful output
docs.text:
Choose early whether the first useful version of your pipeline is:

  • vector only
  • raster only
  • tabular plus geometry
  • mixed raster and vector

That choice determines which shared helpers you will reuse.

docs.title: Step 2: Start with the processing function
docs.text: Write the core logic first. Make it clear what inputs the function needs and what output it returns before you add publication concerns.

docs.title: Step 3: Wrap it in a task or callable entry point
docs.text:
Depending on the use case, follow one of these patterns:

  • direct function for shell use
  • Celery task for async execution
  • DRF view in computing/api.py for HTTP callers

docs.title: Step 4: Reuse shared integration helpers
docs.text:
Do not re-invent:

  • naming conventions
  • asset path generation
  • GeoServer upload paths
  • layer metadata persistence

Those are already shared across the stack.

docs.title: Step 5: Document the pattern, not just the result
docs.text:
A good pipeline page should tell future contributors:

  • where the code lives
  • what entry point calls it
  • whether it is vector, raster, or mixed
  • which shared helpers it relies on
  • what cloud dependencies are required for full integration
  • how an operator obtains the gee_account_id if the pipeline needs one

docs.title: Good Pages to Borrow From
docs.text:
If you are building a new pipeline, compare these examples:

If you need... Start from a vector clipping workflow Drainage Lines a raster clipping workflow Catchment Area a table-plus-geometry enrichment workflow Facilities Proximity mixed raster and vector publication Stream Order time-series processing ideas NDVI Time Series

docs.title: Incremental Adoption
docs.text:
A new contributor does not need to build the whole cloud publication chain on day one.

A practical way to start is:

  1. understand an existing pipeline page in this section
  2. prototype the data logic locally
  3. expose a direct function or shell workflow
  4. add API or Celery integration
  5. add GEE, GeoServer, or STAC publication only when the science is stable

That staged approach is the same one reflected across the builder pages in this site: start with direct execution, then expose stable entry points, then add publication and metadata integration.

docs.title: Admin Boundary Module
docs.text: File: computing/misc/admin_boundary.py

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This module handles administrative boundary processing for geographic data, specifically extracting tehsil/block-level boundaries and synchronizing them across multiple geospatial platforms.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Processes Indian administrative boundaries (state → district → tehsil/block hierarchy) and distributes them to: - Google Earth Engine (GEE) - Cloud-based geospatial analysis platform - GeoServer - Open-source geospatial data server - Database - Layer metadata storage - STAC catalog - SpatioTemporal Asset Catalog specifications

docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Celery Task Triggered] --> B[ee_initialize]
    B --> C[clip_block_from_admin_boundary]
    C --> D{Census 2011 Data Available?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Filter by TEHSIL column]
    D -->|No| F[Load SOI Tehsil Data]
    F --> G{Census data exists?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Intersect SOI with Census]
    G -->|No| I[Use SOI boundary directly]
    E --> J[Build FeatureCollection]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[create_shp_files]
    K --> L[Generate Shapefile]
    L --> M{GEE Asset Exists?}
    M -->|No| N[upload_shp_to_gee]
    M -->|Yes| O[make_asset_public]
    N --> O
    O --> P[save_layer_info_to_db]
    P --> Q[push_shape_to_geoserver]
    Q --> R{Success?}
    R -->|Yes| S[Generate STAC specs]
    S --> T[Update sync status]
    T --> U[Return True]
    R -->|No| V[Return False]

docs.title: Agroecological Space Module
docs.text: File: computing/misc/agroecological_space.py

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This module generates agroecological zone data for specific administrative blocks by clipping pan-India agroecological classification data to the region of interest (ROI).

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Extracts agroecological zone information for micro-watersheds (MWS) and distributes to: - Google Earth Engine (GEE) - Vector asset storage - GeoServer - Visualization via WMS/WFS (workspace: agroecological) - Database - Layer metadata storage

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Celery Task Triggered] --> B[ee_initialize]
    B --> C[Build ROI Asset ID]
    C --> D[Load MWS FeatureCollection]
    D --> E[Load Pan-India Agroecological Data]
    E --> F[Filter by ROI bounds]
    F --> G[Spatial Join with ROI]
    G --> H[Add UID to features]
    H --> I[Export to GEE Asset]
    I --> J[Check Task Status]
    J --> K{Asset Exists?}
    K -->|Yes| L[save_layer_info_to_db]
    L --> M[make_asset_public]
    M --> N[sync_fc_to_geoserver]
    N --> O{Success?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Update sync status]
    P --> Q[Return True]
    O -->|No| R[Return False]

docs.text:

Returns: bool - Whether layer was successfully synced to GeoServer

docs.title: 1. ROI Definition
docs.text:
roi_asset_id = get_gee_asset_path(state, district, block) + "filtered_mws_{district}_{block}_uid"
Uses pre-computed MWS boundaries with unique identifiers.

docs.title: 2. Data Source
docs.text:
pan_india_asset_id = f"{GEE_EXT_DATASET_PATH}/Agroecological_space_pan_india"
Loads pan-India agroecological classification from external dataset.

docs.title: 3. Spatial Processing
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Pan-India Data] --> B[filterBounds]
    C[MWS ROI] --> B
    B --> D[Clipped Features]
    D --> E[Spatial Join]
    E --> F[Add UID]
    F --> G[Final FeatureCollection]

Spatial Join Logic: - Uses ee.Filter.intersects() with leftField=".geo" and rightField=".geo" - Joins clipped data with ROI features to associate each agroecological feature with its containing MWS - Extracts uid from matched ROI feature and adds to clipped feature

docs.title: 4. UID Assignment
docs.text:
def add_uid(feature):
    feature = ee.Feature(feature)
    roi_match = ee.Feature(feature.get("roi_match"))
    uid = roi_match.get("uid")
    return feature.set("uid", uid).set("roi_match", None)

docs.title: Aquifer Vector Module
docs.text: File: computing/misc/aquifer_vector.py

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This module generates aquifer vector layers by processing principal aquifer data and calculating intersection statistics with micro-watersheds (MWS). It provides detailed aquifer characteristics including yield values, lithology, and groundwater potential.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Processes aquifer boundary data to: - Calculate intersection areas between aquifers and MWS - Determine aquifer classification (Alluvium vs Hard-Rock) - Compute weighted yield contributions - Distribute to GEE, GeoServer, and STAC

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Celery Task Triggered] --> B[ee_initialize]
    B --> C[Load MWS ROI]
    C --> D[Load Principal Aquifers]
    D --> E[Map Yield Values]
    E --> F[Filter Valid Yields]
    F --> G[Process Each MWS Feature]
    G --> H[Find Intersecting Aquifers]
    H --> I[Calculate Intersection Area]
    I --> J[Compute Weighted Yield]
    J --> K[Determine Aquifer Class]
    K --> L[Build Feature Properties]
    L --> M[Flatten Results]
    M --> N[Export to GEE]
    N --> O{Asset Exists?}
    O -->|Yes| P[save_layer_info_to_db]
    P --> Q[make_asset_public]
    Q --> R[sync_fc_to_geoserver]
    R --> S[Generate STAC]
    S --> T[Update Sync Status]
    T --> U[Return True]

docs.title: 1. Yield Value Mapping
docs.text:
The module maps textual yield descriptions to numerical values:

yield_dict = ee.Dictionary({
    "": "NA",
    "-": "NA",
    "Upto 2%": 0.02,
    "1-2%": 0.02,
    "Upto 1.5%": 0.015,
    "Upto 3%": 0.03,
    # ... more mappings
    "8 - 16%": 0.16,
    "Not Explored": "NA",
    # ... additional mappings
})

docs.title: 2. MWS Processing
docs.text:
For each MWS feature:

flowchart LR
    A[MWS Geometry] --> B[Filter Aquifers by Bounds]
    B --> C[For Each Aquifer]
    C --> D[Calculate Intersection]
    D --> E[Compute Area]
    E --> F[Calculate Weighted Yield]
    F --> G[Determine Class]
    G --> H[Build Feature]

docs.title: 3. Aquifer Classification
docs.text:
aquifer_class = ee.Algorithms.If(
    ee.String(principal_value).equals("Alluvium"), 
    "Alluvium", 
    "Hard-Rock"
)

docs.title: 4. Feature Properties
docs.text:
Each output feature contains:

Property Description uid MWS unique identifier id Feature ID area_in_ha MWS area in hectares intersection_area_ha Aquifer-MWS intersection area %_area_aquifer Percentage of MWS covered by aquifer weighted_contribution Weighted yield contribution aquifer_count Number of aquifers intersecting MWS aquifer_class "Alluvium" or "Hard-Rock" Age Geological age Lithology_ Lithology code Major_Aq_1 Major aquifer code Major_Aqui Major aquifer name Principal_ Principal aquifer type avg_mbgl Average depth to water level m2_perday Specific capacity m3_per_day Yield in m³/day y_value Numerical yield value

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Facilities Proximity module processes and analyzes facility proximity data for villages across India. It provides distance metrics from villages to various essential facilities including healthcare, education, agriculture, banking, and food distribution centers.

docs.title: Primary Data Source
docs.text:
• CSV File: data/closest_facilities/closest_facilities.csv
  • Contains village-level distance metrics to 25+ facility types
  • Identifier: censuscode2011 (primary key for joining)
  • Location hierarchy: state → district → subdistrict (tehsil) → name (village)

  • Located in data/admin-boundary/input/
  • Joined with CSV data using censuscode2011
