docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
The module handles the following error scenarios:

  1. Missing CSV File: Raises FileNotFoundError with descriptive message
  2. No Villages Found: Returns status "no_data" with message
  3. Missing Geometries: Falls back to point geometries from lat/lon in CSV
  4. Join Failures: Logs warning and continues with available data
  5. GEE Upload Failures: Logs error and continues with local processing

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This document explains how to set up and troubleshoot Google Earth Engine (GEE) authentication for the CoreStack backend application.

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
When running Celery tasks that use Google Earth Engine, you may encounter the following error:

GEEAccount.DoesNotExist: GEEAccount matching query does not exist.

This error occurs when: 1. The GEEAccount table in the database is empty 2. The GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID in .env points to a non-existent account 3. The gee_account_id parameter passed to a task is invalid or None

docs.title: Automated Setup (Recommended)
docs.text:
Use the test_gee_auth.py script to automatically test your GEE credentials and set up Django:

# From WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux)
cd /mnt/y/core-stack-org/core-stack-backend
/home/amitportal/miniconda3/envs/corestack-backend/bin/python test_gee_auth.py --json /path/to/service-account.json --setup

docs.title: Manual Setup
docs.text:
If you prefer to set up manually:

  1. Start Django shell: 

    python manage.py shell

  2. Create GEE Account: 

    from gee_computing.models import GEEAccount
from nrm_app.settings import FERNET_KEY
from cryptography.fernet import Fernet
import json

# Read your service account JSON
with open('/path/to/service-account.json', 'r') as f:
    creds = json.load(f)

# Encrypt credentials
fernet = Fernet(FERNET_KEY)
encrypted = fernet.encrypt(json.dumps(creds).encode())

# Create account
account = GEEAccount.objects.create(
    name='default',
    service_account_email=creds['client_email'],
    credentials_encrypted=encrypted,
)
print(f"Created account with ID: {account.id}")

  3. Update .env file: 

    GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID=<account_id_from_step_2>

  4. Restart Celery worker: 

    celery -A nrm_app worker -l info -Q nrm

docs.title: test_gee_auth.py
docs.text: A standalone script that tests GEE authentication and can automatically configure Django.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
# Interactive mode
python test_gee_auth.py

# Test with JSON file only
python test_gee_auth.py --json /path/to/key.json

# Test and auto-setup Django (recommended)
python test_gee_auth.py --json /path/to/key.json --setup

# Only check Django database status
python test_gee_auth.py --check-only

# Custom account name
python test_gee_auth.py --json /path/to/key.json --setup --account-name my-account

docs.title: Command Line Arguments
docs.text: Argument Short Description --json -j Path to service account JSON key file --setup -s Automatically set up Django with credentials --account-name -n Name for the GEE account (default: "default") --check-only -c Only check Django database, skip credential test

docs.title: What the Script Does
docs.text:
1. Tests GEE Authentication:
  2. Validates the JSON credentials structure
  3. Creates OAuth2 credentials object
  4. Initializes Earth Engine

  5. Tests basic operations (fetching Delhi boundary, simple calculation)

  6. Sets Up Django (with --setup flag):

  7. Encrypts credentials using FERNET_KEY
  8. Creates GEEAccount record in database

  9. Updates .env file with GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID

  10. Provides Diagnostic Information:

  11. Lists all GEE accounts in database
  12. Shows which account is set as default
  13. Identifies missing or misconfigured accounts

docs.title: 1. utilities/gee_utils.py - Enhanced ee_initialize() function
docs.text:
The function now handles missing accounts gracefully:

def ee_initialize(account_id=GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID):
    # Use default account if account_id is None or invalid
    if account_id is None:
        account_id = GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID

    try:
        account = GEEAccount.objects.get(pk=account_id)
    except GEEAccount.DoesNotExist:
        # Fall back to default account if the specified account doesn't exist
        if account_id != GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID:
            print(f"GEEAccount with id={account_id} not found, falling back to default account")
            account = GEEAccount.objects.get(pk=GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID)
        else:
            raise

    # ... rest of the function

This change: - Uses GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID when account_id is None - Falls back to default account if specified account doesn't exist - Provides helpful error messages for debugging

docs.title: Error: "GEEAccount matching query does not exist"
docs.text:
Cause: No GEE accounts in database or invalid GEE_DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID

Solution: Run the setup script: 

python test_gee_auth.py --json /path/to/key.json --setup

docs.title: Error: "PermissionDenied"
docs.text:
Cause: Service account not registered with Earth Engine

Solution: 1. Go to https://code.earthengine.google.com/register 2. Register the service account email for Earth Engine access

docs.title: Error: "Invalid Credentials"
docs.text:
Cause: JSON key file is corrupted or service account was deleted

Solution: 1. Go to Google Cloud Console 2. Create a new service account key 3. Download and use the new JSON file

docs.title: Error: "Network Error"
docs.text:
Cause: Cannot connect to Google servers

Solution: 1. Check internet connection 2. Verify firewall allows HTTPS to: - earthengine.googleapis.com - oauth2.googleapis.com

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
1. Never commit service account JSON files to version control
  2. Add JSON files to .gitignore: 
    # Service account keys
*.json
!package.json
!package-lock.json
  3. The credentials are encrypted before storing in the database using the FERNET_KEY from your .env file
  4. Delete temporary credential files after setup: 
    rm gee_test_credentials.json

docs.title: HLS Interpolated NDVI Module
docs.text: File: computing/misc/hls_interpolated_ndvi.py

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This module provides utility functions for generating gap-filled, interpolated NDVI time series from Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel (HLS) data. It is a supporting module used by ndvi_time_series.py.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Creates high-quality NDVI time series by: - Combining Landsat and Sentinel data - Gap-filling with MODIS data - Temporal interpolation for missing values - Producing 14-16 day composite intervals

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[HLS L30 + S30 Data] --> B[Calculate NDVI]
    B --> C[Create 16-day Composites]
    C --> D[Pair with MODIS]
    D --> E[Linear Regression]
    E --> F[Gap Fill with MODIS]
    F --> G[Mask Low QA]
    G --> H[Temporal Interpolation]
    H --> I[Interpolated NDVI Time Series]

docs.title: 1. get_hls_collection()
docs.text:
Location: Line 221

Fetches and merges HLS Landsat (L30) and Sentinel (S30) collections.

Processing:

docs.title: 3. Combine_LS_Modis()
docs.text:
Location: Line 119

Pairs HLS data with MODIS for gap-filling.

Processing: 1. Renames HLS bands with _lsc suffix 2. Pairs each HLS image with corresponding MODIS image (±8 days) 3. Performs linear regression between HLS and MODIS 4. Applies gap-filling using regression model

docs.title: 4. gapfillLSM()
docs.text:
Location: Line 74

Performs actual gap-filling using MODIS data.

Logic: 

# Linear regression model
offset = LSC_modis_regression_model.select("offset")
scale = LSC_modis_regression_model.select("scale")

# Gap-filled value
modisfit = image.select(modis_bandName).multiply(scale).add(offset)
gapfill = lsc_image.unmask(nodata).where(mask.Not(), modisfit)

Quality Weights: | MODIS QA Value | Weight | Description | |----------------|--------|-------------| | 0 | 0.8 | Good data | | 1 | 0.5 | Marginal data | | 2+ | 0.2 | Snow/ice or cloudy |

docs.title: 5. interpolate_timeseries()
docs.text:
Location: Line 186

Performs temporal interpolation on gap-filled time series.

Processing: 1. Masks low-quality pixels (QA = 0.2) 2. Adds timestamp band to each image 3. Uses spatial-temporal join to find before/after images 4. Performs linear interpolation between valid observations

Interpolation Formula: 

timeRatio = (t - t1) / (t2 - t1)
interpolated = beforeMosaic + (afterMosaic - beforeMosaic) * timeRatio

The hydrology pipeline uses the SCS-CN (Soil Conservation Service Curve Number) method for runoff estimation, the industry standard for watershed hydrology.

docs.title: SCS-CN Method
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Rainfall] --> B{Runoff<br/>Calculation}
    C[Curve Number] --> B
    D[Initial<br/>Abstraction] --> B

    B --> E[Runoff Depth]

docs.title: Key Equations
docs.text:
Potential Maximum Retention: 

S = (25400 / CN) - 254

Runoff Depth: 

Q = (P - Ia)² / (P - Ia + S)    for P > Ia
Q = 0                             for P ≤ Ia

Where: - Q = Runoff depth (mm) - P = Rainfall depth (mm) - Ia = Initial abstraction (typically 0.2S) - S = Potential maximum retention (mm) - CN = Curve Number (0-100)

docs.title: 2. Curve Number Calculation
docs.text:
# Calculate area-weighted CN
for mws in micro_watersheds:
    cn_values = []
    areas = []

    for lulc_class, soil_group in zip(lulc, soils):
        cn = lookup_cn(lulc_class, soil_group)
        area = get_area(lulc_class)
        cn_values.append(cn * area)
        areas.append(area)

    weighted_cn = sum(cn_values) / sum(areas)

docs.title: 3. Runoff Calculation
docs.text:
def calculate_runoff(precipitation_mm, curve_number):
    """Calculate runoff depth using SCS-CN method."""
    if curve_number == 0:
        return 0

    # Maximum retention
    S = (25400 / curve_number) - 254

    # Initial abstraction
    Ia = 0.2 * S

    # Runoff depth
    if precipitation_mm <= Ia:
        return 0

    numerator = (precipitation_mm - Ia) ** 2
    denominator = precipitation_mm - Ia + S

    return numerator / denominator

docs.title: 4. Peak Discharge
docs.text:
Using the Rational Method:

Qp = CiA

Where: - Qp = Peak discharge (m³/s) - C = Runoff coefficient - i = Rainfall intensity (mm/hr) - A = Catchment area (km²)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This module generates natural depression raster layers by clipping pan-India depression data to specific block boundaries. Natural depressions are important for water harvesting and groundwater recharge planning.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Processes natural depression data for: - Water harvesting site identification - Groundwater recharge potential assessment - Watershed management planning - Distributes to GEE, GeoServer (via GCS), and database

docs.text:
NDVI is masked by land cover class:

flowchart LR
    A[LULC Image] --> B[Extract Class Masks]
    B --> C[Crop Mask: classes 8,9,10,11]
    B --> D[Tree Mask: class 6]

    C --> F[Apply to NDVI]
    D --> F

LULC Classes: | Class | Description | |-------|-------------| | 6 | Tree cover | | 8, 9, 10, 11 | Crop types | | 12 | Shrub cover |

docs.text:
# Get interpolated NDVI time series
ndvi = get_padded_ndvi_ts_image(f_start_date_str, f_end_date_str, roi, 14)

# Add masked bands for each class
def add_masked_bands(img):
    nd = img.select("gapfilled_NDVI_lsc")
    date = img.date().format("YYYY-MM-dd")

    return ee.Image.cat([
        nd.updateMask(crop_mask).rename(ee.String("crop_").cat(date)),
        nd.updateMask(tree_mask).rename(ee.String("tree_").cat(date)),
        nd.updateMask(shrub_mask).rename(ee.String("shrub_").cat(date)),
    ])

docs.title: NREGA Assets Module
docs.text: File: computing/misc/nrega.py

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This module processes NREGA (National Rural Employment Guarantee Act) assets data - geospatial point data representing work sites under India's rural employment scheme. It clips district-level NREGA data to block boundaries and synchronizes across GEE, GeoServer, and STAC.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Extracts NREGA work site locations for specific administrative blocks and distributes them to: - Google Earth Engine (GEE) - Cloud-based geospatial analysis platform - GeoServer - Open-source geospatial data server (workspace: nrega_assets) - Database - Layer metadata storage - STAC catalog - SpatioTemporal Asset Catalog specifications

docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Celery Task: clip_nrega_district_block] --> B[Initialize GEE]
    B --> C[Connect to AWS S3]
    C --> D[Load NREGA District GeoJSON from S3]
    D --> E{Geometry exists?}
    E -->|No| F[Create Point geometry from lat/lon]
    E -->|Yes| G[Proceed with existing geometry]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Load SOI Tehsil Boundary]
    H --> I[Filter SOI by state/district/block]
    I --> J[Dissolve to get block boundary]
    J --> K[Spatial Filter: NREGA points within block]
    K --> L[Clean column names & handle NaN]
    L --> M[Save as Shapefile]
    M --> N{GEE Asset Exists?}
    N -->|No| O{File size > 10MB?}
    O -->|Yes| P[upload_shp_to_gee]
    O -->|No| Q[export_vector_asset_to_gee]
    P --> R[Check task status]
    Q --> R
    N -->|Yes| S[Skip upload]
    R --> T{GEE Asset Exists?}
    S --> T
    T -->|Yes| U[save_layer_info_to_db]
    U --> V[make_asset_public]

    W --> X{Success?}
    X -->|Yes| Y[Generate STAC specs]
    Y --> Z[Update sync status]
    Z --> AA[Return True]
    X -->|No| AB[Return False]

docs.title: Entry Pattern
docs.text:
LCW conflict is a good example of the CoRE Stack "thin API, heavy task" pattern.

computing/api.py
@api_view(["POST"])
@schema(None)
def generate_lcw(request):
    try:
        state = request.data.get("state").lower()
        district = request.data.get("district").lower()
        block = request.data.get("block").lower()
        gee_account_id = request.data.get("gee_account_id")
        generate_lcw_conflict_data.apply_async(
            args=[state, district, block, gee_account_id], queue="nrm"
        )
        return Response({"Success": "Successfully initiated"})
    except Exception as exc:
        return Response({"Exception": exc})

That handler maps to POST /api/v1/generate_lcw/, and the queued task then executes the pan-India clip, UID join, GEE export, GeoServer sync, and metadata save.

computing/misc/lcw_conflict.py
@app.task(bind=True)
def generate_lcw_conflict_data(self, state, district, block, gee_account_id):
    ee_initialize(gee_account_id)
    roi_asset_id = (
        get_gee_asset_path(state, district, block)
        + "filtered_mws_"
        + valid_gee_text(district.lower())
        + "_"
        + valid_gee_text(block.lower())
        + "_uid"
    )
    pan_india_asset_id = f"{GEE_EXT_DATASET_PATH}/lcw_conflict_pan_india"
    description = f"{valid_gee_text(district.lower())}_{valid_gee_text(block.lower())}_lcw_conflict"
    asset_id = get_gee_asset_path(state, district, block) + description
    roi = ee.FeatureCollection(roi_asset_id)
    pan_india_data = ee.FeatureCollection(pan_india_asset_id)
    clipped_data = pan_india_data.filterBounds(roi.geometry())
    ...
    clipped_data_with_uid = joined_data.map(add_uid)
    task = export_vector_asset_to_gee(clipped_data_with_uid, description, asset_id)
    ...
    if is_gee_asset_exists(asset_id):
        layer_id = save_layer_info_to_db(...)
        make_asset_public(asset_id)
        res = sync_fc_to_geoserver(...)

For a new pan-India clip module, this is usually the fastest pattern to copy: keep the route small, keep the pipeline task self-contained, and reuse the shared helpers rather than writing publication code from scratch.

docs.title: 1. ROI Definition
docs.text:
roi_asset_id = (
    get_gee_asset_path(state, district, block)
    + "filtered_mws_"
    + valid_gee_text(district.lower())
    + "_"
    + valid_gee_text(block.lower())
    + "_uid"

docs.title: 2. Spatial Filter
docs.text: clipped_data = pan_india_data.filterBounds(roi.geometry())

docs.title: 3. Spatial Join (Add UID)
docs.text:
spatial_filter = ee.Filter.intersects(
    leftField=".geo", rightField=".geo", maxError=1
)

join = ee.Join.saveFirst(matchKey="roi_match")
joined_data = join.apply(clipped_data, roi, spatial_filter)

def add_uid(feature):
    feature = ee.Feature(feature)
    roi_match = ee.Feature(feature.get("roi_match"))
    uid = roi_match.get("uid")
    return feature.set("uid", uid).set("roi_match", None)

clipped_data_with_uid = joined_data.map(add_uid)

for arg in args:

    value = ee.Number(value).multiply(0.0001)

def calculate_intersection(soge_feature):

docs.title: Slope Calculation
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[3x3 Window] --> B[dz/dx]
    A --> C[dz/dy]

    B --> D[Slope = atan√(dz/dx² + dz/dy²)]
    C --> D

Using Horn's method for improved accuracy:

def calculate_slope(dem):
    """Calculate slope in degrees from DEM."""
    # Calculate gradients
    dz_dx = ((dem[0,0] + 2*dem[0,1] + dem[0,2]) -
             (dem[2,0] + 2*dem[2,1] + dem[2,2])) / (8 * cell_size)

    dz_dy = ((dem[2,0] + 2*dem[1,0] + dem[0,0]) -
             (dem[2,2] + 2*dem[1,2] + dem[0,2])) / (8 * cell_size)

    # Calculate slope
    slope_rad = np.arctan(np.sqrt(dz_dx**2 + dz_dy**2))
    slope_deg = np.degrees(slope_rad)

    return slope_deg

    A[dz/dx] --> B[Aspect = atan2(dz/dy, -dz/dx)]
    A --> C[dz/dy]
    C --> B
    B --> D[Convert to 0-360]
    D --> E[North = 0°]

Aspect represents the compass direction that a slope faces:

Aspect Range Direction 0-22.5, 337.5-360 North 22.5-67.5 Northeast 67.5-112.5 East 112.5-157.5 Southeast 157.5-202.5 South 202.5-247.5 Southwest 247.5-292.5 West 292.5-337.5 Northwest

docs.title: Hillshade
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Slope] --> D[Hillshade = 255 × (cos(Z) × cos(S) + sin(Z) × sin(S) × cos(Az-As))]
    B[Aspect] --> D
    C[Sun Azimuth] --> D
    C2[Sun Zenith] --> D

Default sun position: Azimuth = 315°, Zenith = 45°

docs.title: API Errors Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for common error shapes and categories documented for the CoRE Stack API.

docs.title: Error Response Format
docs.text:
All errors should be understandable enough for both API users and maintainers to debug quickly.

{
  "status": "error",
  "error": {
    "code": "ERROR_CODE",
    "message": "Human-readable description",
    "details": {},
    "request_id": "req-uuid-for-support"
  }
}

docs.title: Error Categories
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[API Errors] --> B[Client Errors 4xx]
    A --> C[Server Errors 5xx]

    B --> D[400 Bad Request]
    B --> E[401 Unauthorized]
    B --> F[403 Forbidden]
    B --> G[404 Not Found]
    B --> H[429 Rate Limited]

    C --> I[500 Internal Error]
    C --> J[502 Bad Gateway]
    C --> K[504 Timeout]

docs.title: INVALID_PARAMETER
docs.text:
The request contains invalid or missing parameters.

{
  "status": "error",
  "error": {
    "code": "INVALID_PARAMETER",
    "message": "Invalid or missing required parameters",
    "details": {
      "state": "State 'unknown' not found",
      "year": "Year must be between 2015 and 2024"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: INVALID_JSON
docs.text: The request body contains malformed JSON.

docs.title: VALIDATION_ERROR
docs.text: Request parameters failed validation rules.

docs.title: MISSING_FIELD
docs.text:
Required field is missing from the request.

Field Description state State name is required district District name is required block Block name is required year Year is required for temporal analyses

docs.title: UNAUTHORIZED
docs.text:
Request lacks valid authentication credentials.

Typical fixes:

  • add X-API-Key for public data routes
  • add Authorization: Bearer ... for JWT-protected routes
  • confirm which auth mode the target route actually uses

docs.title: INVALID_API_KEY
docs.text: The provided API key is invalid or malformed.

docs.title: EXPIRED_TOKEN
docs.text: The provided bearer token has expired and needs refresh or re-login.

docs.title: INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS
docs.text:
The caller is authenticated but not allowed to perform the requested action.

Common causes:

  • a user is outside the required organization scope
  • a route requires superadmin or organization admin privileges
  • a project assignment does not match the user's organization

docs.title: RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND
docs.text:
The requested route, object, or dataset does not exist.

Typical examples:

  • wrong district or block name
  • wrong organization or project identifier
  • stale layer or dataset URL

docs.title: RATE_LIMITED
docs.text:
The caller has sent too many requests in a short period.

Typical response guidance:

  • retry after waiting
  • reduce polling frequency
  • batch requests where possible

docs.title: INTERNAL_ERROR
docs.text: The backend failed in an unexpected way.

docs.title: UPSTREAM_FAILURE
docs.text: A dependency such as GeoServer, Earth Engine, or another external system failed or returned an invalid response.

docs.title: TIMEOUT
docs.text: The backend or an upstream job exceeded a time limit.

docs.title: Practical Debug Order
docs.text:
1. Confirm the route exists in API Overview.
  2. Confirm the expected auth mode in Authentication.
  3. Check spelling and case for geographic names and IDs.
  4. For internal task flows, compare against Run Internal Tasks.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: This page is the starter reference for CoRE Stack terms, common request fields, spectral indices, and raster band vocabulary. It will grow into a broader field and layer dictionary over time.

docs.title: Core CoRE Stack Terms
docs.text: MWS Micro-Watershed. A small watershed unit used for planning, hydrology, and reporting. LULC Land Use Land Cover. A classification of how land is being used and what physically covers it. SWB Surface Water Body. A mapped pond, tank, lake, or related water feature. STAC SpatioTemporal Asset Catalog. A standard way to describe and discover geospatial assets and metadata. GEE Google Earth Engine. A cloud platform for large-scale geospatial analysis. GeoServer A server used to publish geospatial layers and services such as WMS and WFS. JWT JSON Web Token. A signed bearer token used for authenticated API access. API key A simpler credential, often sent as X-API-Key, for selected public data routes. Local-First A mode or design goal where users can inspect, fetch, and process data locally instead of depending entirely on cloud execution.

docs.title: Common Request and Dataset Fields
docs.text:
These are starter meanings for fields that appear repeatedly across current docs and API examples.

Field Meaning Typical use state State name spatial filtering and task submission district District name spatial filtering and task submission block Block name spatial filtering and task submission tehsil Tehsil or sub-district name public data queries and layer discovery latitude North-south coordinate point-based lookup routes longitude East-west coordinate point-based lookup routes start_year first year in an analysis window temporal compute jobs end_year last year in an analysis window temporal compute jobs gee_account_id backend-side Earth Engine account reference GEE-backed computations layer_url a published layer endpoint or derived layer link map consumption and download flows geojson_path stored path or JSON reference to geometry data projects and vector outputs app_type project application domain, such as watershed project and organization workflows

Note

This is not yet a full schema dictionary for every layer. We will extend this section with field-by-field layer and dataset tables as those references are stabilized.

docs.title: Geospatial Concepts
docs.text: Raster A grid of pixels or cells, where each cell stores a value such as reflectance, elevation, or class. Vector Geometry-based data represented as points, lines, or polygons. CRS Coordinate Reference System. Defines how coordinates map onto the earth. EPSG code A standard numeric identifier for a CRS, such as EPSG:4326. DEM Digital Elevation Model. A raster representing elevation. Spectral index A derived value computed from one or more raster bands to highlight a property such as vegetation vigor or water. False color composite An image where displayed colors do not match natural human vision, often used to emphasize vegetation or moisture. Runoff Water that flows across land after rainfall instead of infiltrating into the soil. Evapotranspiration Combined water loss from soil evaporation and plant transpiration.

docs.title: Common Spectral Indices
docs.text: Index Formula Typical use NDVI (NIR - Red) / (NIR + Red) vegetation vigor and greenness NDWI (Green - NIR) / (Green + NIR) open water detection MNDWI (Green - SWIR) / (Green + SWIR) water extraction in built-up or noisy scenes EVI 2.5 * (NIR - Red) / (NIR + 6*Red - 7.5*Blue + 1) vegetation monitoring in dense canopies NDBI (SWIR - NIR) / (SWIR + NIR) built-up area highlighting

docs.title: Common Raster Band Definitions
docs.text: Band family What it often helps show Blue haze, shallow water, sediment, coastal features Green vegetation reflectance, water visibility, visual interpretation Red chlorophyll absorption, vegetation contrast NIR biomass, vegetation health, land-water separation SWIR1 moisture, soil and vegetation water content SWIR2 dryness, burn scars, mineral and moisture contrasts

docs.title: Common Sensor Band Mappings
docs.text: Spectral region Sentinel-2 Landsat 8/9 Blue B2 B2 Green B3 B3 Red B4 B4 NIR B8 B5 SWIR1 B11 B6 SWIR2 B12 B7

Good for contributors who want a structured introduction to HTML, CSS, Git, Python, Django, SQL, JavaScript, testing, and security.

Why it fits CoRE Stack:

  • makes Django shell and model concepts less intimidating
  • helps explain why routes, serializers, migrations, and auth work the way they do
  • useful for students who want a deeper software foundation

docs.title: Spatial Thoughts
docs.text: An open learning platform for modern geospatial technologies with strong coverage of QGIS, Python, Google Earth Engine, and GDAL.

docs.title: QGIS Training Manual
docs.text: The official QGIS step-by-step manual. Useful if you want to inspect layers, styles, attributes, and raster outputs beyond API responses.

docs.title: NASA Earthdata: Remote Sensing Basics
docs.text: A good primer on how remotely sensed imagery is interpreted, including color, context, and feature identification.

docs.title: NASA ARSET
docs.text: NASA's Applied Remote Sensing Training Program offers free beginner-to-advanced training across agriculture, disasters, ecological conservation, and water resources.

docs.title: Calculating Spectral Indices for Land and Aquatic Applications Using QGIS
docs.text: Useful when you want a concrete tutorial around indices such as NDVI and related land or water workflows.

docs.title: awesome-gee-community-catalog
docs.text:
A community-maintained catalog for Earth Engine-ready datasets that complements the main Earth Engine catalog.

Why it fits CoRE Stack:

  • helps researchers discover datasets they can query or adapt
  • gives newcomers examples of how public geospatial data is packaged and documented
  • can inspire future CoRE Stack layer references and examples

docs.title: USGS Water Science School
docs.text: Useful for foundational concepts around the water cycle, groundwater, surface water, and water quality.

docs.title: Hydrology Pipeline Docs
docs.text: Read this alongside the USGS material when you want to connect general hydrology concepts with CoRE Stack-specific workflows.

docs.title: ARSET agriculture trainings
docs.text: The current ARSET catalog includes agriculture-oriented material on crop mapping, evapotranspiration, soil moisture, and large-scale remote sensing workflows.

docs.title: Use Remote Sensing Data to Study Vegetation Dynamics
docs.text: Helpful for students or analysts trying to understand vegetation monitoring and why indices like NDVI matter.

docs.title: FAO elearning Academy
docs.text: A broad training portal for agriculture, food systems, and related domains.

docs.title: FAO: Remotely Sensed Information for Crop Monitoring and Food Security
docs.text: Useful when you want agriculture-oriented framing for how remote sensing supports crop monitoring and food security.

Read it when you want to think about:

  • what participatory and democratic design should mean
  • why documentation, transparency, and community stewardship matter

docs.title: Watershed Data Model
docs.text:
This page presents a conceptual watershed-management data structure for CoRE Stack.

It is meant to help students, researchers, geospatial scientists, and builders understand how hydrological, administrative, and programmatic entities relate to each other across the platform.

Note

This is a conceptual reference model for documentation and system understanding. It is not a claim that the current backend exposes this exact structure as one single Django ORM schema.

docs.title: Availability Legend
docs.text: Category Meaning GEE asset primarily thought of as a geospatial asset or derived layer in Earth Engine or related geospatial publishing flows Existing API currently useful as an API-facing concept in CoRE Stack docs and routes Programmatic access typically obtained or enriched through scripts, tasks, or internal processing

docs.title: Entity Availability
docs.text: Entity Availability lens River Basin GEE asset Sub-basin GEE asset Watershed GEE asset Micro-watershed Existing API and analysis-facing entity Waterbody GEE asset and derived analysis output State GEE asset and API-facing lookup concept District GEE asset and API-facing lookup concept Tehsil Existing API-facing administrative unit Village GEE asset and enriched analysis unit NREGA Asset Programmatic access and derived vector layer

docs.title: Hydrological Hierarchy
docs.text:
Level Typical role in docs and analysis River Basin top-level hydrological grouping Sub-basin intermediate hydrological grouping Watershed watershed-scale planning or aggregation unit Micro-watershed main planning, reporting, and pipeline-analysis unit Waterbody feature-level hydrological object nested inside or related to MWS workflows

The most important practical unit for current CoRE Stack documentation is the micro-watershed, because many analyses, joins, and layer outputs eventually resolve to that level.

docs.title: Administrative Hierarchy
docs.text:
Level Typical role in docs and analysis State top-level API filter and asset path component District common query, routing, and publication scope Tehsil important boundary and publication scope Village detailed demographic and facility-enrichment unit

This hierarchy is often the entry point for users, while the hydrological hierarchy is often the analysis frame.

docs.title: Cross-Hierarchy Relationships
docs.text:
Three relationships matter especially for users reading CoRE Stack docs:

  1. Tehsil to Micro-watershed: many analyses are triggered or published by an administrative region but computed on hydrological units.
  2. Micro-watershed to Village: facility, demographic, and planning layers often intersect or aggregate across both.
  3. Micro-watershed connectivity graph: drainage and downstream relationships matter for runoff, waterbody, and watershed analysis.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
This structure helps explain why CoRE Stack documentation repeatedly moves between:

  • geographic API lookups such as state, district, and block
  • hydrological units such as MWS and waterbodies
  • enriched planning layers such as NREGA assets, aquifer data, and terrain outputs

If a reader understands this page, the pipeline and API docs become much easier to navigate.

docs.title: Python Testing Script
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import json
import requests


class CoreStackAPITester:
    def __init__(self, base_url="http://127.0.0.1:8000", api_key=None):
        self.base_url = base_url.rstrip("/")
        self.session = requests.Session()
        if api_key:
            self.session.headers["X-API-Key"] = api_key

    def test_states(self):
        response = self.session.get(f"{self.base_url}/api/v1/get_states/")
        print(f"Status: {response.status_code}")
        print(json.dumps(response.json(), indent=2))
        return response

    def test_generated_layer_urls(self, state, district, tehsil):
        response = self.session.get(
            f"{self.base_url}/api/v1/get_generated_layer_urls/",
            params={"state": state, "district": district, "tehsil": tehsil},
        )
        print(f"Status: {response.status_code}")
        print(json.dumps(response.json(), indent=2))
        return response
